responses.text: The target article accurately defines the Mandela Effect as a phenomenon where a large group of people share a false memory of an event or detail.
The term was coined in 2009 by Fiona Broome, a paranormal consultant, who discovered that many others shared her false memory that Nelson Mandela died in prison in the 1980s.
In reality, Nelson Mandela was released from prison, subsequently became the President of South Africa, and lived until 2013.
While some conspiracy theorists attribute this widespread misremembering to alternate realities or parallel universes, scientific consensus points to psychological mechanisms.
Psychologists suggest that confabulation plays a significant role, where the brain unconsciously fills in memory gaps to create what feels like a complete sequence of events.
Cognitive science research confirms that human memory is highly reconstructive, meaning that schema-driven expectations and social reinforcement can alter how memories are recalled.
Research specifically focusing on the visual Mandela Effect has demonstrated that certain cultural icons and logos systematically and reliably elicit shared false memories.
Ultimately, the claims outlined in the article serve as well-documented illustrations of how pliable, imperfect, and suggestible human memory can be.
